title: 2025/10/15 - MA
displayTitle: 2025-10-15 - Māori Affairs Committee - Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework)
topic: Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework)
description: Wednesday 15 October 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework) Hearing of evidence Submitter List: Te Puni Kōkiri Melissa Ross, Deputy Secretary, Strategy (Acting) Grace Smith, Deputy Secretary Regions Paula Rawiri, Deputy Secretary, Policy Karen McGuiness, General Manager, Investments Bernard (Bern) Te Paa, General Manager, Wellbeing, Policy Joanne (Jo) Tuapawa, General Manager, Strategy Kelsey Jones, Regional Director, Waikato-Waiariki (acting) Debra Anderson, Manager, Organisational Performance Kylie Hood, Principal Advisor, Investments
descriptionSummary: Briefing on Te Tautuhi-o-Rongo (Māori Macro Policy Framework). Te Puni Kōkiri. Melissa Ross, Deputy Secretary, Strategy (Acting)

chunks.text: Hi,
silence.

Just as a little bit of a opening, uh, we had a great visit to, uh, is really cool when you saw some, uh, great examples with, uh, Tahi as well as RA with what they're doing there. And obviously, uh, it's, it shows that the, the, the policy, the program that you've got there is, is working really well. So I just want to allay the, perhaps come the farm.

This is, this is really just filling the gap that perhaps we perhaps didn't quite get the opportunity to discuss with, uh, the people that were on the ground. And, and that's just basically closing the loop from the policy here in Wellington and the delivery of the outcomes out in the field.

And we've seen both those sides of it. So really, uh, today's conversation is, uh, uh, regional delivery and, uh, you know, looking at, at questions such as how are you identifying the entities that you, you choosing to talk to and in an enabling and, and the whole process through that localness part of it, but the actual, uh, the policy, you know, the, uh, uh, framework, we understand, uh, what that's all about.

Uh, so just with that, we, we, what have we got here? We've got, uh, according to me, we have got half an hour, uh, with you itself. So, uh, maybe you wanna make some opening comments before we perhaps get into a little bit of a discussion about it. So where you go?

I'm the Deputy Secretary Strategy. Uh, I'm here with my colleagues, uh, Kelsey Jones. She's the regional director from Waikato and Karen McInnis, who is the general manager of our investments. And, uh, part of those investments that we saw on the ground, uh, behind us.

Uh, we are also joined by other leadership from, uh, uh, today continues that, uh, as you mentioned on our approaches. And, uh, in July we did talk about our policy approach and then, um, our delivery approach in, uh, on the, on the ground.

And we can begin with a, a recap, uh, very short. Sure. Um, and then Kelsey will talk to the work, uh, that she does in the region. And then Karen can also talk, um, to the work in her role and that she's based here. But there we do, uh, both investments, um, regionally and nationally as well.

Correct. And we can use those two examples we saw to, to discuss that. Thank you. Uh, and we'll reinforce this. There is a no wrong door approach. And then, uh, I think once we've talked to, um, to that, we can pause, uh, and then, um, happy to take the, any questions that we may have missed from there.

Uh, there were some, uh, slides, but it was, um, more just to recap there we have our two approaches and, uh, you, I've mentioned those. Our delivery approach, uh, which is whanau centered, locally led and government enabled, and our policy approach of TU or, uh, in ua, you meet the two entities, uh, Tora North and Tahi Kii.

Uh, they demonstrate what is possible when Maori led solutions are backed by targeted government support. And tki, HC was the first entity we visited. Uh, and, um, there are Maori land, uh, incorporation and they administer 22 land blocks for over a thousand shareholders.

And Tora North is AUA trust, uh, with significant land holdings, primarily northeast of to near Lake awa. Uh, Kelsey and Karen will provide more details, uh, on those. Uh, so I will, um, hand over to Kelsey to continue Got it. Ua, uh, today I'll briefly highlight how our approaches were applied to the, uh, to the sites that you visited in August for delivery of whanau centered, locally led government enabled.

These investments reflect our approach because they are wau centered by supporting Maori ownership and expertise. They're locally led, driven by strong governance, and they're government enabled with investment, exhilarating process progress, and unlocked broader opportunities.

These examples show how our approach delivers real impact that are Maori led, sustainable and grounded in whanau aspirations for both to North and tki. The focus was on overcoming long standing barriers to investment, such as limited access to capital regulatory complexities and gaps in technical capabilities.

Our support enabled them to navigate consenting processes, secure expert advice, and attract investment to unlock economic potential on the ua. Both proposals reflect issues faced by Maori as citizens that is ensuring their access to equal opportunity.

Um, as you know, that is, um, Maori Investment Consortium. It's led by to North. Um, they've, um, browed our investment and is made up of the commercial arms of 19 iwi trusts and other Maori entities. So the collective approach that they have aims to address the systemic barriers to Maori investments, such as the high transaction costs, limited cash reliance on consultancy firms and lack of access to expertise.

This is particular case for some of those smaller iwi. You have, um, very limited funds on their own to, um, invest on behalf of their beneficiaries. So the Maori Development Fund, um, co-invested just under a million dollars with a similar investment from the til collective for a 12 month pilot.

Um, so how we worked, uh, this initiative was worked up at a national level in line with our no wrong door approach, but through the relationships that we'd built up across the mortar with the founding groups that make up to, so along with tota, that's the likes of au au, um, we had na, awa, um, some of those type entities and also with New Zealand Trade and Enterprise.

So New Zealand trade and Enterprise had taken, um, a collective of, um, iwi and Land Trust to a big investment conference. And, and part of that, they came up with a barrier of actually how do the collective eyes to be able to come together to invest, to have more impact. Um, so we worked alongside them and NZTE to see how could Bernie come in and enable that investment, um, to bring the coalition of the willing together, so to speak, other agencies were not able to provide that funding support, um, that TY can to actually build the capability of those entities.

Um, this is really important across all our investments. Um, we need to make sure that we're not duplicating what other agencies can do. Mm-hmm. Um, we thought it was a really cost effective way of working with the collective to address those common barriers that they all face. The pilot was successful, the membership of the collective went from six originals to 19 members with a strong shared investment pipeline.

Um, to date around a million dollars of in public investment has unlocked a hundred million dollars in New Zealand owned infrastructure, um, with more deals still in progress. So that's infrastructure that's going to stay in New Zealand hands with the collective on the board of the entity talk to the members have grown and measurably from peer support and learning from each other's successes and their challenges.

It's a real ka Maori approach, um, learning by actively participating in deals. Um, we were thinking forward when we, um, worked into this investment. Um, we included an evaluation as part of the process to ensure that we could get insights that could inform policy, but also we could, um, work out how they could work towards becoming self-sufficient.

So self-sustaining, um, relying on less government funding for that capability support. We, we'll just go back to, um, Kelsey, who's gonna talk more about doing the role as well. So why Taki eight C? So Taki eight C is a proven Maori Landon corporation with a strong delivery record and a clear vision for economic transformation.

The Green Energy Hub includes a number of bold multi-phase energy initiatives, such as geothermal, hydro, and the opportunity for diesel production. Grounded in UA Maori co-invested $810,000, and to date TKI eight C had contributed over 3 million of their own skin in the game.

The project has the opportunity with a potential to bring $125 million of international investment and unlock 1,300 jobs. Additionally, it will provide 85% of the region's electricity demand in the region that imports 80% of its electricity.

This initiative directly aligns with our investment priority one, improving productivity of Maori asset and supports broader government goals around energy, resilience, job creation, and regional development. Our regional lead engagement, we are from the community.

We know the community needs, we know the system and we support. We are supported by our national team who provide policy and a coordinated expertise. We support Iwi, hapu, and Maori communities to navigate the system through brokering, through connecting with other agencies to help unlock opportunities.

We facilitate connections with economic agencies such as NPI, kawa, NCTE to align opportunities and to unlock co-investment. This funding for TKI eight C has enabled TKI to work through the fast check approval process, the RMA process and support stakeholder consultation and prepare key documents.

This is a clear example of how Whanau centered locally led government enabled supports or enables Maori led infrastructure that delivers long-term regional benefits. Um, yeah, you wanna, we'll pause there. Okay. Uh, if you've got some questions Sure.

Just before too, can you pop Outside and just ask the, sorry, a bit noisy. Don't get It. Hey, thank you very much. It's, it, it's great. And just a, a bit of a starter, uh, for me. Um, clearly you've got some, uh, we, we've, we've observed two successful, quite large entities.

You know, we're talking, uh, it's not, it's not small stuff. So is there a threshold for where the framework works and where it doesn't work? Like, do you understand the question there? Mm-hmm. It's almost like, uh, what are we actually, uh, reaching out to and what are we not reaching out to? Mm-hmm.

Um, the, there is no, the, the threshold that we have with our investment plan is a minimum investment of a hundred thousand. So these, these ones are quite large, so a million, um, and over 800 for, for, um, Tahiti Sea. So we're aiming at around about a hundred thousand because we think that's kind of the sweet spot because we're trying to, in order to make a funding go further, um, we're also, and pick the ones that we know are gonna show return on investment.

Um, we do, um, require some co-investment from, from Mount Kai, from those we're investing in. Right. Um, so it's generally, um, there are some exceptions, but generally you're trying to work with, if we put a hundred thousand dollars in, we'd expect a hundred Thousand. So how would you describe on what Maori entities are not reaching the, how would you bracket that?

So, so basically, um, I think what you've described is you have to be quite a size before you can actually tap into this. I'm trying to identify what, what is missed out on your, So, I, I think in terms of what we're trying to reach with our investment plan, so our, so our, um, investments are anchored off our investment plan.

So investment plan is really much about, there are two priorities here. One, the first one is improving the productivity of iwi or Maori assets and co-invest and infrastructure. So, so that's, um, they're generally investments around a hundred thousand dollars or over. So it's things like, um, increasing the productivity of UA or other Maori assets and closer diversification into new areas or, um, such as renewable energy, um, really trying to, um, increase the utilization or underperform performing assets.

So they have to come to the table with some capital. It's a, you're leveraging, uh, that they've had. Yes. And, and, but generally too, we're trying to pathway them on to larger investment. Mm-hmm. So for the rif, for example, um, or other private investment. So helping kind of build the, the business case like, in order to be able to do that.

So they've generally got something, but some of our iwi who are a little bit smaller or haven't settled, then we will look at that, we'll look at some inkind support or what they may have put in in the future in, sorry, in the past. Okay. At the regional level, Kelsey, uh, do you see that there's some entities that's got potential but are not quite, you know, are there, do you, um, observe missed opportunities with how the framework's set itself because of, because of that, you know, those thresholds?

Yes, we do. Um, and I guess that's where the point of, uh, the opportunity such as collective and the collective advising of entities coming together, that's a, um, at a regional level because of our local intelligence and our ability to, one, be from the community, but also, uh, provide a, a facilitation of bringing entities together.

Yes. To answer your question, yes, there are times, but we work with entities to bring them together, such as to collective. Yeah. I'm sorry. The other thing I might excuse to say is that we do have a portion of our funding, well, these two, um, investment priorities focus on economic development. We do also have, um, a portion of, of funding that we use for what we call community resilience.

So that can be kind of to build the capability of groups to get there. A lot of that focus is on marae, um, has been on emergency preparedness, it's been on and skills and development, and, and also providing the ability for regions who, who know what the needs aspirations are in each region to work out the proposals that, that align with their regional priorities, the communities.

Um, thanks Mr. Chair. Um, and thanks for pointing out the difference in, um, the biggies versus the average Maori land trust. Um, because there's a significant difference in the challenges that we face. Um, and you've spoken to the community resilience fund, which is good for community and for marai.

What specifically in the f maori space do we have in terms of that? So you've got scale with the biggies, like the, and then you've got some like kind, uh, stronger ones, but not quite to that scale. Do you have, um, that UA Maori funding that can help those who are sort of sweet spot middle trying to elevate and get a bit more to scale?

Can they be prioritized within the funding that We're talking about? Yes, they can. Yeah. I think, um, it's important for me to hear that, that the, um, the entities that you visited were the big ones. Um, yeah, they're big. They, they were our kind of shiny light ones. The ones that it's easy to say, Hey, this is the return on investment. But definitely what we're trying to do is, is support those smaller and middle ones to get there.

So We also have, um, made an investment with, to help a number of smaller, um, uh, funeral blocks to go through the pre-feasibility feasibility business case. And that's been going for around about 12 months. And there's a number of those blocks that in our investment ready for other, um, agencies to pick up the support or private investors.

Um, are you able to give us an overview in terms of the allocation of spend regionally? So that we can see, you know, maybe is benefiting heaps, could all be in district.

Oh, We do have that. Um, so from last year, um, the majority, so there was around about 13 million, um, the MA actually about 8 million we took, um, the remaining, um, regions had between three to 5 million per region. How much do you think, uh, governance, uh, capacity quality governance as a constraint for where that spends going itself?

Because we have a, a whole lot of different maturities mm-hmm. Of, of the entities and I think one of the, uh, areas that the committee's interested, building the capacity, building the governance capacity, sometimes just starting a governance for an entity, how much do, do you see that as that one of the bigger constraints for being able to, uh, step into the conversations regionally with other entities itself? Comments on that?

Uh, we do have some in kind, um, examples as well. Are you able to talk to some of the in kind governance training? So you are looking at building governance capability within entities as well. You, you, you are proactively funding on funding that as well. Mm-hmm.

We funded that through, uh, a range of, um, resources so we can use community resilience fund funding for smaller entities. Yep. Even, um, a portion of talk about it was going towards governance around, um, informed decision making. Yeah. And, um, yeah, generally the governance funding, um, is funded through the community resilience.

It'll do it. No, I'm nervous. Um, uh, That's better. Thank you. It's very, yeah. Wanting to un tap off that as well, because, um, for instance, you might have those that aren't necessarily startup completely, but um, you know, they might wanna get a master plan

for their, um, those feasibilities to then do something with it. Um, your existing f Maori service is still strong and available and capable. Uh, we, we, our Fen Maori service, um, essentially all our staff in our regions are, are expected to be able to work in, in that fen space.

We do have some staff in each region too. It's Been generalized now, right? Yeah. But we do have staff in regions who have, do have specific skills and say, um, business development around exporting focus, but also around, um, they're in our regions, but they, we expect our staff to be able to work on a whole range of things.

But, but definitely, um, ENO is always a, a focus for us no matter what you're doing. So we are from the uri, we know the community needs, and often in a regional level, we work in those spaces. So again, um, we're able to, uh, bring in our high trust relationships work closely, whether it's governance, capability, uplift, and what we do is we provide the insight, we gather the regional, um, insight, and we provide it to our national team, whether that's providing, um, insightful policy, whether that's providing insight for our, uh, investment strategy.

So that's one part. The next part, uh, another separate to that is being able to, uh, support our, um, Maori groups to navigate the system. Yeah. Whether that is beyond investment of our own capability of the, of us as an investment, the regional coma here as an investment, as well as working alongside our other, um, agencies such as NPI, Kawa Corp, And that reach back, I think into, we do have a lot of those skills in the, um, in the head and the national, at the national level as well.

And if we need to, then we can, uh, support those regions with what they need. And also, I think really importantly is trying to coordinate across agencies, because if we can all invest in the same projects to accelerate and not every agency can invest. So for example, NPI will provide, um, expert advice on where to put a PAC Farm, um, but I can't necessarily and invest in it, and we can come in and support some of that due diligence.

Mm-hmm. Um, and then attracting investors, um, as local investors, NZTE, but they also have Invest New Zealand. I Was just gonna ask about that. Mm. Yeah. 'cause the coordination and I, the navigation point of TPK is really needed in the regions. Mm. Because often whanau don't know how to go from place one place to another.

Um, in respect to what you're seeing and how you are feeling, um, you know, that desire for navigation are government departments open to, you know, sitting down and having some meetings to try and have a bit of not shared services, but coordinate better the way that you can go out in a joint way in a joined up approach.

Can we just expand on that? Are they proactive and come to you? Yeah. Yeah. They already did. Yes. Definitely. And, and, uh, um, and Kelsey can talk to this from a regional level, but actually it, it's at a regional level. Those agencies need to get together. Um, and, and Kelsey's region, they all meet over their proposals.

And then a national level we need to coordinate as well. 'cause some of the things we are finding is that, um, from a regional level, these projects are guys, once they get to an agent, some agencies national level, but not even on the pipeline. So It's really quiet. Mm-hmm. Yes. So, so we are working and, and our agencies, if they, a lot of them are coming into our national office because, um, some of the, particularly the really tricky kind of, um, larger investments that coming in national office, but the first thing we are

doing is, is following it back out through our regional office and coordinating regional and national, um, across all agencies. Okay. Um, at a regional level, what occurs is we have a, um, a collective of agencies made up of Kawa, N-P-I-N-Z-T-E and invest in New Zealand.

Um, and we meet every month to discuss pipelines to discuss the opportunity. We look for things like, what is the return on investment for our region? What is the opportunity for Maori to, to invest in this? And it's not looking at Maori as, uh, ticking in, in the consenting process.

It's actually looking at Maori as co-investors to the opportunity. And so as agencies, we meet together to identify the opportunity. Mm-hmm. Very good. So, um, amongst some of the, uh, challenges that you've identified, Kelsey, about the likes of capital constraints, you also talked about likes of, uh, regulatory constraints as well.

So, uh, are there regulatory, uh, impediments or challenges or hurdles, uh, that are getting in the way of your progress? I'm trying to see what is stopping you from going faster mm-hmm. This Good mahi that you're doing. Mm-hmm. Okay. Uh, so in terms of regulatory issues, it's around, um, legislation such as, so the complexity of, of the consenting process and enabling Maori to participate in that.

Um, and the delay of that progress. We also talk about the complexities of UA Maori and it being multiply owned. Yep. And the, um, and its ability not to be able to, or sorry, not to be able to, but, um, uh, barriers to development and the struggle of development versus, uh, maintaining the UA in a space.

Uh, the other part goes back to the point of governance. Yes. Governance capability. Yeah. Yeah. I think that's also why our policy colleagues are so important to, to feed. And that's the beauty about having those high trust relationships is, is when you invest in a community, you, you are, you're in there for the, the long haul where you're actually, you get invited to the meetings, you, you, you're collecting those insights.

Mm-hmm. You, uh, you are feeding them through. Um, so that's a, a policy colleagues in national office, we are feeding those policy insights through to them directly from our Kai tunnels, um, to work through some of those barriers with other agencies policy, um, And, um, the secretary policy.

So just No, no, that's right. Its, um, so just as an example, if we think about the recent, uh, engagement around, uh, in relation to the amendments to, one of the things that we were able to do because of the regional connections is look to, uh, those exemplar organizations or look to them through the relationships to people who we would really like to hear from, make sure that we hear from, with respect to those amendments.

And I could see that tki, HC and, um, Taha North both provided, um, uh, input into, through the, through the consultation process. Yeah. Uh, with relation, in relation to those amendments. And so some of the examples that they pulled out, um, in relation to those proposed amendments were things like governance.

Um, so that they could, they could see that as the proposals while saying, um, that it would create greater autonomy, um, for governance entities. What it might, what the proposals might also do is create some, uh, instability or uncertainty because there was going to a proposal to lower the threshold of, uh, shareholder initiated investigations, as well as the ability for the Maori land court to be able to, um, uh, seek an investigation.

So their feedback was, that's, uh, kind of seems to be counter to, um, what you are trying to, um, achieve, which is greater autonomy for the particular entity. Yep. Um, similarly, there was, uh, discussions, uh, support for, there was a proposal in the current amendments, which was around, um, 99 year leases.

And one of the entities actually said that, that would be much better for us, um, to be able to do that. We wouldn't have to have the court, um, involved in that decision making. So we do support that proposal. So, um, by undertaking the work that we do through the investments and so forth, it also has another additional piece, which is we reach out to those entities and say, what do you think about those proposals?

And we can, we can take some of those forward as part of our advice to government. Excellent. Well, unfortunately, our times come to an end, but I think, uh, today has been very valuable. It's just, just filled that little gap that we're missing as a committee. And can I just pass on, um, congratulations, uh, for the framework that, that this is, and, and Kelsey, for the work that we observe there in, in rot, uh, of what that's delivering, it's, it's, it's, uh, hitting the mark of what we see is really positive.
